jokes.text: Oh, too cool for street insurance? Must be nice.
jokes.setup: Someone suggests asking about insurance 'on the street'
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic response treating 'street insurance' as a luxury item
jokes.explanation: Solid absurd logic joke with good economy. The idea of 'street insurance' as a status symbol is unexpectedly funny.

jokes.text: No, that's Furio, my gay son. Him, I get.
jokes.setup: Hickey mentions his son having a kid and someone assuming he saw this son at a wedding
jokes.punchline: Casual correction that he has TWO sons and only understands one of them
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment. The casual 'Him, I get' reveals Hickey's relationship struggles while being unexpectedly progressive.

jokes.text: I'm sensing an emergency collage situation.
jokes.setup: Hickey mentioning his grandson's birthday
jokes.punchline: Annie's over-eager response using 'collage' instead of 'college' situation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Annie - over-eager helper energy combined with malapropism

jokes.text: 'Dungeons and Dragons'? - That's the crap.
jokes.setup: Jeff clarifies what Hickey was referring to
jokes.punchline: Hickey's blunt dismissal of the game they're about to use to help him
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective. Hickey's dismissive attitude toward the very thing meant to help him is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Was everyone's takeaway last time that we use D&D to reprogram brains? Not that we almost caused a suicide?
jokes.setup: Group enthusiastically agrees to play D&D to help Hickey
jokes.punchline: Abed points out their last D&D game nearly ended in tragedy
jokes.explanation: Perfect meta-commentary on the show's own history. Dark humor that acknowledges consequences while being genuinely funny.

jokes.text: And thanks to us, he's still out there, doing this and that in the background.
jokes.setup: Defending their previous D&D intervention
jokes.punchline: Vague, dismissive description of Neil's current existence
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dismissive way to acknowledge a character who was supposedly 'saved' but clearly forgotten.

jokes.text: Satisfying sequel's difficult. Many geniuses defeated themselves through hubris. Making this a chance to prove I'm better. I'm in!
jokes.setup: Group discussing another D&D game
jokes.punchline: Abed's meta-commentary on sequels that becomes personal competitive drive
jokes.explanation: Perfect Abed - academic film analysis transforming into personal creative ego

jokes.text: Dungeons. It'd be the dungeons.
jokes.setup: Hank asks what appeals to his father about D&D at age 60
jokes.punchline: Hickey's overly literal, simplistic answer
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hickey response - practical, literal-minded, completely missing the point of his son's question.

jokes.text: Oh, that's just what I love about role-playing games is being told exactly what to do.
jokes.setup: Abed gives detailed quest description with specific goal
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic complaint about the rigid structure
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Hank's resistance to authority, even in a game context.

jokes.text: Joseph Gordon Diehard. Really? Son of Sir Riggs Diehard. Well, I'm Sir Riggs Diehard, so I guess I'm your dad in the game too.
jokes.setup: The contrived father-son character pairing in the game
jokes.punchline: Hickey's matter-of-fact acceptance makes it more awkward
jokes.explanation: The awkwardness is the joke. Hickey's blunt acknowledgment makes the contrived setup even more uncomfortable.

jokes.text: Oh, boy. Guys, I don't suppose this is some contrived paint-by-numbers adventure that's designed to force an emotional bond between me and my emotionally stunted father, is it?
jokes.setup: The obviously contrived father-son setup
jokes.punchline: Hank directly calling out the transparent manipulation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-moment where the character explicitly identifies the sitcom plot device. 'Paint-by-numbers' is a perfect description.

jokes.text: I'm a holy cleric with a mace and a dumb name.
jokes.setup: Character introductions after reshuffling
jokes.punchline: Jeff's dismissive description of his own character
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jeff Winger response - acknowledging the silliness while participating. 'Dumb name' is wonderfully dismissive.

jokes.text: I'm Tiny Nuggins, a thief, and the rest is gibberish.
jokes.setup: Character introduction continuation
jokes.punchline: Annie's practical dismissal of fantasy complexity
jokes.explanation: Good character moment. Annie cuts through the fantasy jargon with typical efficiency.

jokes.text: You think that's a weird coincidence? I'm a troll named Dingleberry.
jokes.setup: Discussion of character name coincidences
jokes.punchline: Chang's escalation to an even more ridiculous name
jokes.explanation: Chang escalating the coincidence complaint with an even sillier name

jokes.text: That's my mom's nickname for me.
jokes.setup: Chang gets the character name 'Dingleberry'
jokes.punchline: Reveals this embarrassing coincidence
jokes.explanation: Classic Chang overshare. The coincidence is mildly funny but relies on crude humor.

jokes.text: "for we believe man's greatest weapon against evil...is the bond...Is the bond between father and son."
jokes.setup: Hank reading his character's backstory
jokes.punchline: The on-the-nose therapeutic messaging making everyone uncomfortable
jokes.explanation: The uncomfortable pause and repetition showing how heavy-handed the therapeutic setup is

jokes.text: I walk too far south, do I fall off your graph paper there?
jokes.setup: Hickey wants to explore beyond the preset path
jokes.punchline: Calling out the artificial boundaries of the game world
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke about D&D's literal limitations. 'Graph paper' perfectly captures the artificial nature of the game world.

jokes.text: No. It's not a competition. The only winner is fun. We're having fun.
jokes.setup: Hickey asks if they're losing
jokes.punchline: Jeff's increasingly strained insistence that this is fun
jokes.explanation: Good escalation as Jeff tries to maintain the therapeutic facade while chaos builds.

jokes.text: I go to Tristram, and I'm laughing and I pick him up, and I carry him to the bridge. It's fun.
jokes.setup: Jeff says they're having fun
jokes.punchline: Dean's forced, manic attempt to demonstrate 'fun'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dean moment - his desperate need to please makes 'fun' feel threatening and awkward.

jokes.text: His body becomes wreathed in white hot flame, burning Hector for...Six damage.
jokes.setup: Jeff's character casting a spell to avoid being carried
jokes.punchline: The dramatic escalation to actual violence
jokes.explanation: Sudden escalation from forced fun to magical violence, disrupting the session

jokes.text: Would've been constructed in the third age by goblins. They used primitive iron anchors.
jokes.setup: Asked if he miscalculated bridge strength
jokes.punchline: Abed's detailed justification for why the bridge had to collapse
jokes.explanation: Perfect Abed - using elaborate world-building to justify what was clearly plot manipulation.

jokes.text: He's not your grandson. He's my son. And I didn't invite you to the birthday party because I wanted to enjoy it.
jokes.setup: Hickey complains about not being invited to grandson's birthday
jokes.punchline: Hank's brutal honesty about why he wasn't invited
jokes.explanation: Devastating honesty that's funny because it's so direct. The family dysfunction becomes real and painful.

jokes.text: We're not going to continue. Thank you very much, guys. This was just awesome.
jokes.setup: The D&D game has devolved into family argument
jokes.punchline: Hank's sarcastic thanks as he tries to leave
jokes.explanation: Standard sarcastic exit line. Works in context but not particularly inventive.

jokes.text: If I kill the necrophile before you do I come to my grandson's birthday.
jokes.setup: Hickey proposes a competition
jokes.punchline: Mispronounces 'necromancer' as 'necrophile'
jokes.explanation: Classic malapropism that fits Hickey's character perfectly. Simple but effective character comedy.

jokes.text: Well, I'm assuming you guys can help me beat him...because if we lose, I'm gonna punch each of you in the heart.
jokes.setup: Hickey addressing his team
jokes.punchline: Threatening violence as motivation for teamwork
jokes.explanation: Hickey's twisted approach to team leadership and motivation

jokes.text: Hey, I didn't scamper in the jungles of Nicaragua and I won't do it now.
jokes.setup: Abed says Tiny Nuggins 'scampers' into jungle
jokes.punchline: Annie's oddly specific military reference
jokes.explanation: Unexpected military reference from Annie is both funny and hints at her overachiever background research.

jokes.text: Larger, stronger, and worth more in Scrabble than regular goblins.
jokes.setup: Explaining what hobgoblins are
jokes.punchline: Scrabble point reference breaks fantasy immersion
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke that breaks the fourth wall by referencing Scrabble points for 'hob'. Very Abed.

jokes.text: There are no women with them, are there? I got class.
jokes.setup: About to charge into combat with hobgoblins
jokes.punchline: Chang's misplaced chivalry in violent fantasy context
jokes.explanation: Chang's confused sense of honor and propriety in completely inappropriate context

jokes.text: Go find a name that's not just another creature's name plus 'hob.'
jokes.setup: Defeating hobgoblins
jokes.punchline: Jeff's taunt reveals the lazy naming convention
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-commentary on D&D creature naming. Jeff calling out fantasy tropes mid-combat is perfectly written.

jokes.text: And I may be gone, but just remember whenever the wind whispers through the woods you got me killed.
jokes.setup: Shirley's character dies and she gives a touching farewell
jokes.punchline: The beautiful moment turns accusatory
jokes.explanation: Perfect subversion of the noble death speech. Shirley's passive-aggressive guilt trip disguised as poetry is brilliant.

jokes.text: I've punched about a thousand hearts in my life. I never, never missed.
jokes.setup: Hickey's character missing an attack
jokes.punchline: Hickey's absurd boast about his perfect heart-punching record
jokes.explanation: Escalation of his earlier threat with specific statistics. The precision of 'a thousand' and 'never missed' makes the absurd claim funnier.

jokes.text: I take a moment of silence to mourn my fallen comrade, Crouton before rifling through her belongings.
jokes.setup: Respectful mourning of fallen party member
jokes.punchline: Immediately follows with pragmatic looting
jokes.explanation: Perfect D&D player behavior - fake sentiment followed by immediate selfishness. The juxtaposition is expertly timed.

jokes.text: Times Square?
jokes.setup: Hank says Hickey wasn't there for birthdays, was at 'a place that rhymes with not there'
jokes.punchline: Annie's innocent misinterpretation of what rhymes with 'not there'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection. Annie's innocent guess avoids the obvious 'nightmare' for something completely unexpected but technically correct.

jokes.text: Back, eight-legged demons! I will not scoop you up with a catalogue and let you outside on this day!
jokes.setup: Sky Spiders appear and Hank draws his sword
jokes.punchline: Hank's battle cry references mundane spider removal methods
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist humor mixing fantasy heroics with domestic spider-catching reality. 'Catalogue' is a perfect specific detail.

jokes.text: Plus, they eat all the sky mosquitoes.
jokes.setup: Britta explains Sky Spiders aren't dangerous
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to 'sky mosquitoes' as if they're obvious
jokes.explanation: Good world-building absurdity. The casual mention of 'sky mosquitoes' expands the ridiculous ecosystem perfectly.

jokes.text: Hello. You're looking well.
jokes.setup: Jeff casting 'Speak with Monsters' on giant Sky Spiders
jokes.punchline: Polite small talk with terrifying creatures
jokes.explanation: Jeff's characteristic smooth-talking applied to absurd fantasy situation

jokes.text: Damn, you made that Sky Spider your bitch, yo!
jokes.setup: Britta successfully communicates with Sky Spiders through diplomacy
jokes.punchline: Chang's crude modern language breaks fantasy immersion
jokes.explanation: Classic Chang moment - inappropriate modern slang in fantasy context. Effective but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: I was best man at that guy's wedding.
jokes.setup: Hickey trying to drive wedge between goblin prisoners
jokes.punchline: Goblin revealing unexpectedly close friendship
jokes.explanation: Humanizes goblins with relatable friendship detail, subverting expectations

jokes.text: I was best man at that guy's wedding... Your friend says you just got married. Congratulations. Too bad about the food.
jokes.setup: Annie manipulates goblins by claiming they're talking about each other
jokes.punchline: Goblin reveals actual friendship details, Annie continues manipulation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant psychological manipulation sequence. Annie's interrogation technique becomes increasingly absurd but effective.

jokes.text: Come on, between you and me, which one do you think is yours?
jokes.setup: Annie has been manipulating goblin relationships
jokes.punchline: She implies paternity questions about goblin children
jokes.explanation: Dark and brilliant. Annie's manipulation reaches psychological torture levels, completely subverting her innocent character.

jokes.text: Each night, I will think of you and rub...praying for the night fate will find us rubbing together.
jokes.setup: Hank's character writing emotional letter to his father
jokes.punchline: Innocent sword-rubbing ritual described in suggestive terms
jokes.explanation: Perfectly innocent D&D mechanic described in accidentally suggestive terms

jokes.text: Each night, I will think of you and rub praying for the night fate will find us rubbing together.
jokes.setup: Hank writes touching message about magical sword connection
jokes.punchline: The sword-rubbing metaphor becomes uncomfortably sexual
jokes.explanation: Accidental innuendo that creates cringe comedy. Works but relies on juvenile double entendre.

jokes.text: I got a piece of popcorn stuck in my tooth. Like, right under the gum. I've been trying like for 15 minutes with, you know, my tooth and then with my tongue and my finger. I'm always one tooth off, and I just can't seem to... Oh, hello, there we go. Anyway... But it may end in war.
jokes.setup: Dramatic moment about impending conflict
jokes.punchline: Chang interrupts with mundane popcorn problem, then returns to drama
jokes.explanation: Perfect Chang moment - completely derailing dramatic tension with mundane personal issues, then casually returning to drama.

jokes.text: This is a man who thinks of grandchildren like trophies. Which is why he'll play to win. And why I can't let him.
jokes.setup: Hickey explaining why the competition matters
jokes.punchline: Reveals his own competitive obsession while criticizing Hank's
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Hickey's self-awareness and lack of it simultaneously. The irony is well-crafted.

jokes.text: because every man has the right to hang out with his grandson.
jokes.setup: Hickey explaining his motivation to kill the necromancer
jokes.punchline: Framing family access as fundamental rights issue
jokes.explanation: Hickey's self-righteous framing of what's essentially his own failure as absent father

jokes.text: I hug my father! - It's a trick! - I'm not letting him hug me. - I hug my father! - He has gunpowder in his pants.
jokes.setup: Touching moment as Hank tries to hug his father
jokes.punchline: Paranoid reactions treat the hug as combat
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation where a genuine emotional moment becomes paranoid combat. The 'gunpowder in pants' detail is perfect absurdity.

jokes.text: Worth it.
jokes.setup: Hank's character being impaled while hugging his father
jokes.punchline: Simple acceptance that the emotional connection was worth dying for
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character moment - dying for a hug was worth it

jokes.text: I owe you nothing. I am a Dungeon Master. I create a boundless world, and I bind it by rules. Too heavy for a bridge? It breaks. Get hit? Take damage. Spend an hour outside someone's front door fighting over who gets to kill him? He leaves through the back.
jokes.setup: Players complain about anticlimactic ending
jokes.punchline: Abed's passionate defense of logical consequences
jokes.explanation: Excellent Abed moment that doubles as meta-commentary on storytelling logic. His DM philosophy is both funny and genuinely insightful.

jokes.text: I owe you nothing. I am a Dungeon Master. I create a boundless world, and I bind it by rules.
jokes.setup: Players demanding a proper ending to the adventure
jokes.punchline: Abed's god-complex speech about DM authority
jokes.explanation: Perfect Abed - turning DM role into philosophical statement about creative control

jokes.text: Hey, guys. Take it from a bunch of ghosts. This is no kind of life. You need fresh air and frozen yogurt. It's on me. Hug it out.
jokes.setup: Dead characters giving life advice
jokes.punchline: Shirley's mom-like practical advice from beyond the grave
jokes.explanation: Perfect Shirley moment - even as a ghost, she's offering practical maternal advice and treats.

jokes.text: I also don't think they can handle being apart. And I think they just found a way to avoid doing either.
jokes.setup: Observing Hickey and Hank's continued D&D partnership
jokes.punchline: Realizing they've found the perfect dysfunctional compromise
jokes.explanation: Insightful observation about the psychological solution they've found

jokes.text: And that's the best most fathers and sons can do.
jokes.setup: Observing that Hickey and Hank can neither be together nor apart
jokes.punchline: Jeff's cynical but wise assessment of father-son relationships
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jeff wisdom - cynical but accurate observation about family relationships delivered with characteristic detachment.

jokes.text: Oh, a goblin notices you and murders you all. You're dead. They're all yours.
jokes.setup: Dean's elaborate tea party game
jokes.punchline: Abed's abrupt, violent ending to get rid of annoying players
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending - Abed's brutal efficiency in dealing with the Dean's irritating tea party game. The casual murder is perfectly delivered.
